meta.title: SF-SPC-2: Data Loading, Transformation and Time Travel

questions.stem: Which cloud object storage service does Snowflake use as its storage layer when deployed on AWS?

options.text: Amazon EBS (Elastic Block Store)
options.explanation: EBS is block storage attached to EC2 instances and is not used as Snowflake's data layer.

options.text: Amazon S3 (Simple Storage Service)
options.explanation: Snowflake uses Amazon S3 as its underlying object storage on AWS. Data is stored as compressed, columnar micro-partitions in S3.

options.text: Amazon EFS (Elastic File System)
options.explanation: EFS is a file system service not used by Snowflake's storage layer.

options.text: Amazon Glacier
options.explanation: Glacier is archival storage and not the active storage layer used by Snowflake.

questions.stem: A Snowflake virtual warehouse is set to auto-suspend after 5 minutes of inactivity and auto-resume is enabled. A query is submitted when the warehouse is suspended. What happens?

options.text: The query fails with an error because the warehouse is suspended.
options.explanation: Auto-resume prevents query failures from a suspended state.

options.text: The query is queued for up to 24 hours until the warehouse is manually resumed.
options.explanation: There is no 24-hour queuing behavior for suspended warehouses — auto-resume handles this transparently.

options.text: The warehouse automatically resumes, the query executes, and credits are consumed.
options.explanation: With auto-resume enabled, Snowflake automatically starts the warehouse when a query is submitted, the query executes, and credits are charged for the warehouse uptime.

options.text: The query executes against the Cloud Services layer without consuming warehouse credits.
options.explanation: Most queries require the virtual warehouse compute layer, not just the Cloud Services layer.

questions.stem: Which statement CORRECTLY describes how Snowflake's compute and storage layers interact?

options.text: Multiple virtual warehouses can read from and write to the same storage layer concurrently without contention.
options.explanation: Snowflake's architecture separates storage and compute, allowing multiple virtual warehouses to concurrently access the same shared storage layer. This is a core architectural advantage — different workloads (ETL, reporting, ad-hoc) can run on separate warehouses without contention.

options.text: Each virtual warehouse has dedicated storage that it owns and manages.
options.explanation: Warehouses do not own dedicated storage; they all share the centralized storage layer.

options.text: Only one virtual warehouse can access the storage layer at a time to prevent data corruption.
options.explanation: Snowflake explicitly allows concurrent multi-warehouse access to storage.

options.text: The storage layer is co-located with the compute layer to minimize network latency.
options.explanation: Compute and storage are separate — compute nodes read from object storage (S3/Azure/GCS) over the network.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to track and limit the number of compute credits used by a specific virtual warehouse. Which Snowflake object should they create?

options.text: Network policy
options.explanation: Network policies control IP-based access, not credit consumption.

options.text: Task
options.explanation: Tasks are used to schedule SQL statements, not to limit warehouse credit consumption.

options.text: Row access policy
options.explanation: Row access policies control data visibility in tables, not compute resource usage.

options.text: Resource monitor
options.explanation: A resource monitor allows administrators to define credit quotas and trigger actions (notify, suspend, suspend immediately) when a warehouse reaches defined thresholds. It can be applied at the account or individual warehouse level.

questions.stem: Which TWO of the following are characteristics of Snowflake micro-partitions? (Select TWO)

options.text: Micro-partitions are mutable and are updated in-place when rows change.
options.explanation: Micro-partitions are explicitly immutable.

options.text: Micro-partitions are immutable; updates result in new micro-partitions being created.
options.explanation: and **C** are correct. Micro-partitions are immutable — when data is updated or deleted, Snowflake marks old micro-partitions as deleted and creates new ones, enabling Time Travel. Data within each micro-partition is stored in columnar format, enabling efficient column pruning and compression.

options.text: Data within a micro-partition is stored in columnar format.
Each micro-partition holds exactly 1,000,000 rows.
options.explanation: Micro-partitions do not have a fixed row count — they are sized by compressed data volume (50–500 MB), so the row count varies by data type and compression ratio.

questions.stem: A company is currently on the Enterprise edition and wants to enable cross-region database replication for disaster recovery. Which statement is TRUE?

options.text: Database replication is available on all Snowflake editions including Standard.
options.explanation: Standard edition does not include database replication.

options.text: Cross-region replication requires an upgrade to Business Critical or higher.
options.explanation: Upgrading to Business Critical is not required — Enterprise already supports it.

options.text: Database replication is available on Enterprise edition and above.
options.explanation: Database replication (including cross-region and cross-cloud replication) is available starting with the Enterprise edition. The company on Enterprise edition can enable replication without upgrading.

options.text: Replication is handled automatically by Snowflake for all accounts and cannot be configured.
options.explanation: Replication must be explicitly configured by the account administrator; it is not automatic.

questions.reference: [Introduction to replication and failover across multiple accounts](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/account-replication-intro)

questions.stem: A query result cache hit requires which of the following conditions to ALL be true?

options.text: The same SQL text, the same user (regardless of role), and no micro-partition changes to base tables.
options.explanation: The same user is not sufficient — it must be the same role.

options.text: The same SQL text, the same role, the same warehouse, and no data changes in the base tables within 24 hours.
options.explanation: The same warehouse is not a requirement for result cache hits.

options.text: The same SQL text, the same role, no data changes in the base tables, and the result was cached within the last 24 hours.
options.explanation: The query result cache returns cached results when the exact same SQL text is submitted by a session using the same role, the underlying data has not changed, and the cached result is still within the 24-hour TTL. Notably, the same warehouse is NOT required — the result cache is in the Cloud Services layer and is warehouse-independent.

options.text: The same warehouse and the same SQL text within the same session.
options.explanation: Result cache hits work across sessions, not just within the same session, and warehouse identity is irrelevant.

questions.stem: An X-Large virtual warehouse (16 credits/hr) runs for exactly 45 minutes processing an ETL job. How many credits are consumed?

options.text: 16 credits
options.explanation: 16 credits would be for a full hour.

options.text: 8 credits
options.explanation: 8 credits would be for 30 minutes.

options.text: 12 credits
options.explanation: Credits are billed per second (with a 60-second minimum). An X-Large warehouse costs 16 credits/hr. Running for 45 minutes = 45/60 = 0.75 hours × 16 credits/hr = 12 credits.

options.text: 32 credits
options.explanation: 32 credits would be for a 2XL warehouse running for a full hour.

questions.stem: Which system-defined role should a Snowflake administrator use to create databases, schemas, and warehouses?

options.text: ACCOUNTADMIN
options.explanation: While ACCOUNTADMIN can create these objects, Snowflake best practice recommends using SYSADMIN for object management and reserving ACCOUNTADMIN for account-level administration.

options.text: SECURITYADMIN
options.explanation: SECURITYADMIN manages users and roles, not database objects.

options.text: SYSADMIN
options.explanation: SYSADMIN is the recommended role for creating and managing database objects such as databases, schemas, tables, and warehouses. Using SYSADMIN (rather than ACCOUNTADMIN) follows the principle of least privilege.

options.text: PUBLIC
options.explanation: PUBLIC has minimal privileges and cannot create databases or warehouses.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator executes: `ALTER USER analyst1 SET NETWORK_POLICY = 'corp_policy';`. What is the effect?

options.text: The network policy applies to all users in the account.
options.explanation: Setting a policy on a specific user does not affect all users.

options.text: The command fails because network policies can only be set at the account level.
options.explanation: Network policies CAN be set at both the account level and the individual user level.

options.text: The analyst1 user is removed from the account-level network policy and has no restrictions.
options.explanation: The user's network policy still applies the IP restrictions defined in 'corp_policy' — they are not unrestricted.

options.text: The network policy overrides the account-level network policy for analyst1 only.
options.explanation: A user-level network policy overrides the account-level network policy for that specific user. This allows more granular control — for example, allowing a service account to connect from a different IP range than the corporate policy allows.

questions.stem: A company uses Snowflake Secure Views to share data with external partners. Why is a Secure View required instead of a regular view for data sharing?

options.text: Secure views are the only view type that can be added to a Snowflake SHARE object.
options.explanation: While secure views are required for sharing, the reason is the privacy of the definition, not the exclusivity — it is a policy requirement.

options.text: Secure views encrypt data at rest, which is required for cross-account sharing.
options.explanation: Encryption at rest is handled by Snowflake's storage layer, not by the secure view feature.

options.text: Secure views prevent the consumer from seeing the view definition, protecting proprietary business logic.
options.explanation: Secure views hide the underlying view definition (the DDL) from users who are not the view owner. In a data sharing context, this protects the provider's proprietary business logic and filtering conditions from being inspected by consumers.

options.text: Secure views bypass the query result cache to ensure consumers always see fresh data.
options.explanation: Secure views do interact with the query result cache; bypassing the cache is a different concern.

questions.stem: A row access policy is created with the following function body: `RETURN CURRENT_ROLE() = 'DATA_OWNER' OR t.region = CURRENT_USER()`. This policy is applied to the `sales` table. What does this policy do?

options.text: Users with the DATA_OWNER role see all rows; other users see only rows where the region column matches their username.
options.explanation: The policy returns TRUE (allowing the row) if either the current role is DATA_OWNER (which grants access to all rows) OR if the region column value matches the current username (which restricts other users to only their region's rows).

options.text: Only the DATA_OWNER role can access the table; all other users are denied.
options.explanation: The OR condition means non-DATA_OWNER users can still see rows where their username matches the region.

options.text: Users see all rows if their username matches the region column; DATA_OWNER has no special access.
options.explanation: DATA_OWNER sees ALL rows due to the first condition in the OR.

options.text: The policy returns an error because CURRENT_USER() cannot be compared to a table column.
options.explanation: CURRENT_USER() returning a string is a valid comparison to a string column.

questions.stem: Which TWO authentication methods does Snowflake support for service accounts used in automated pipelines? (Select TWO)

options.text: Username and password with MFA bypass
options.explanation: Password + MFA bypass is not a supported or recommended pattern for service accounts; MFA is a security control that should not be bypassed.

options.text: Key-pair authentication using RSA private/public keys
options.explanation: and **C** are correct. Key-pair authentication is the recommended method for service accounts — the private key is stored securely on the client, and the public key is registered in Snowflake. OAuth (including Snowflake OAuth and external OAuth with providers like Okta) is also supported for programmatic access.

options.text: OAuth access tokens (e.g., Snowflake OAuth or external OAuth)
Biometric fingerprint authentication
options.explanation: Biometric authentication is not a Snowflake authentication method.

questions.stem: A masking policy is applied to the `email` column in the `customers` table. Users with the ANALYST role see `****@****.com` while users with the MARKETING role see the full email address. Which SQL snippet CORRECTLY creates this behavior?

options.text: `CASE WHEN CURRENT_ROLE() IN ('MARKETING') THEN val ELSE '****@****.com' END`
options.explanation: Masking policy bodies use `CURRENT_ROLE()` to determine the active role and return the unmasked value for privileged roles (MARKETING) or the masked value otherwise.

options.text: `CASE WHEN IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION('ANALYST') THEN val ELSE '****@****.com' END`
options.explanation: IS_ROLE_IN_SESSION checks whether the ANALYST role is in session — showing the full value to ANALYST is the inverse of the requirement.

options.text: `CASE WHEN CURRENT_USER() = 'MARKETING' THEN val ELSE '****@****.com' END`
options.explanation: CURRENT_USER() returns the username, not the role, so comparing it to 'MARKETING' would not correctly match the role.

options.text: `IF(CURRENT_ROLE() = 'ANALYST', val, '****@****.com')`
options.explanation: IF() is not standard Snowflake SQL syntax for masking policies; CASE WHEN is the correct pattern.

questions.stem: A data analyst notices that after restarting a virtual warehouse and running their first query, it is slower than subsequent identical queries. What BEST explains this behavior?

options.text: The Cloud Services layer needs to re-authenticate the user after each warehouse restart.
options.explanation: Authentication is handled by the Cloud Services layer and is not affected by warehouse restarts.

options.text: The first query warms up the local disk cache on the warehouse nodes; subsequent queries benefit from cached micro-partition data.
options.explanation: The local disk (SSD) cache on warehouse nodes is empty after a restart. The first query must read micro-partitions from remote object storage (S3/Azure/GCS), which is slower. Subsequent identical queries can read from the populated SSD cache, which is much faster. This is called cache warming.

options.text: The query result cache is cleared when a warehouse restarts, forcing re-computation.
options.explanation: The query result cache is in the Cloud Services layer and is not cleared when a warehouse restarts.

options.text: The optimizer generates a less efficient plan for the first query after a restart.
options.explanation: The optimizer uses the same plan — the difference is the data access path.

questions.stem: A developer runs `EXPLAIN USING TABULAR SELECT * FROM orders WHERE customer_id = 12345`. They see a TableScan node scanning all 10,000 micro-partitions. What is the MOST LIKELY cause?

options.text: The query uses SELECT * which disables partition pruning.
options.explanation: SELECT * does not disable partition pruning — the WHERE clause drives pruning regardless of the SELECT list.

options.text: The EXPLAIN command itself prevents pruning from being evaluated.
options.explanation: EXPLAIN evaluates the query plan including pruning estimates.

options.text: The customer_id column has poor clustering — its values are spread across all micro-partitions.
options.explanation: Snowflake prunes micro-partitions using column min/max metadata. If customer_id values are randomly distributed across all micro-partitions (i.e., the min/max range of every partition includes 12345), no partitions can be eliminated and a full scan occurs.

options.text: The orders table has too few micro-partitions to benefit from pruning.
options.explanation: Having 10,000 micro-partitions is a large table, not too few — and poor clustering on customer_id is the likely culprit.

questions.stem: A materialized view is defined on a base table in Snowflake. The base table receives frequent INSERT and DELETE operations. Which statement CORRECTLY describes how the materialized view is maintained?

options.text: The materialized view is rebuilt from scratch each time the base table changes.
options.explanation: Materialized views are incrementally maintained, not fully rebuilt from scratch.

options.text: The user must manually refresh the materialized view by calling ALTER MATERIALIZED VIEW ... REFRESH.
options.explanation: Snowflake manages automatic refresh — unlike some other systems, users do not need to manually refresh Snowflake materialized views.

options.text: The materialized view becomes stale and is automatically dropped after 7 days without a manual refresh.
options.explanation: Materialized views do not have a 7-day staleness limit or auto-drop behavior.

options.text: Snowflake automatically and transparently maintains the materialized view using background compute, consuming credits.
options.explanation: Snowflake automatically maintains materialized views in the background whenever the base table changes. This maintenance consumes compute credits and happens transparently without user intervention. The optimizer can then use the materialized view to serve queries.

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator is investigating a query that runs for 45 minutes on a Large warehouse. The Query Profile shows that 95% of the time is spent in a HashJoin node. Which TWO actions could potentially improve performance? (Select TWO)

options.text: Increase the warehouse size to XL or larger to provide more memory for the hash join.
options.explanation: and **C** are correct. Hash joins are memory-intensive — a larger warehouse provides more memory per node, reducing disk spilling and improving join performance. Filtering rows earlier (pushing predicates before the join) reduces the data volume the join must process, directly improving performance.

options.text: Enable the Search Optimization Service on the table.
options.explanation: The Search Optimization Service improves point-lookup queries, not join operations.

options.text: Rewrite the query to filter earlier and reduce the number of rows reaching the join.
Create a network policy to restrict concurrent access during query execution.
options.explanation: Network policies control connectivity and have no effect on query execution performance.

questions.stem: The `SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_INFORMATION` function returns `total_partition_count: 5000` and `total_constant_partition_count: 4800`. What does this indicate?

options.text: 4800 micro-partitions are completely prunable for any query on the clustering key.
options.explanation: Constant partitions are well-clustered, not necessarily prunable for all queries — they indicate homogeneity within each partition.

options.text: 4800 micro-partitions have not been reclustered recently and need maintenance.
options.explanation: A high constant partition count indicates good clustering, not maintenance needs.

options.text: 4800 of the 5000 micro-partitions contain only a single distinct value of the clustering key, meaning the table is very well-clustered.
options.explanation: `total_constant_partition_count` represents the number of micro-partitions that contain only a single distinct value of the clustering key column. A high ratio (4800/5000 = 96%) indicates excellent clustering — queries filtering on the clustering key can eliminate nearly all micro-partitions quickly.

options.text: 4800 micro-partitions exceed the recommended maximum size and should be split.
options.explanation: This metric is not related to partition size.

questions.stem: A data engineer wants to load CSV files from their local machine into a Snowflake table. They first need to upload the files to a Snowflake stage. Which stage type allows files to be uploaded directly from a local machine using the PUT command?

options.text: External stage (S3)
options.explanation: You cannot use the PUT command to upload to an external stage — files in external stages must be uploaded via the cloud provider's tools (AWS CLI, Azure Storage Explorer, etc.).

options.text: Internal named stage
options.explanation: The PUT command can upload files from a local machine to an internal stage (user stage `@~`, table stage `@%table`, or internal named stage). Internal stages store files in Snowflake-managed storage.

options.text: External stage (Azure Blob)
options.explanation: is wrong for the same reason as A.

options.text: Table stage using COPY INTO directly
options.explanation: COPY INTO is used to load data from a stage into a table, not to upload files from a local machine.

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to unload data from a Snowflake table to an S3 bucket. Which command accomplishes this?

options.text: `COPY INTO @my_s3_stage FROM orders FILE_FORMAT = (TYPE = CSV);`
options.explanation: `COPY INTO @stage FROM table` is the Snowflake command for data unloading. It exports data from a table or query to files in a named stage (including external stages pointing to S3).

options.text: `EXPORT TABLE orders TO @my_s3_stage;`
options.explanation: EXPORT TABLE is not a valid Snowflake command.

options.text: `INSERT INTO @my_s3_stage SELECT * FROM orders;`
options.explanation: INSERT INTO cannot write to a stage.

options.text: `UNLOAD orders INTO @my_s3_stage;`
options.explanation: UNLOAD is not a valid Snowflake command.

questions.stem: A team is loading 500 GB of gzipped CSV files using COPY INTO. Each file is approximately 2 GB compressed. What is the RECOMMENDED approach to maximize parallel loading throughput?

options.text: Load all files in a single COPY INTO statement and use ON_ERROR = CONTINUE to handle any errors.
options.explanation: Having fewer, larger files limits parallelism regardless of ON_ERROR settings.

options.text: Use a larger warehouse (e.g., 4X-Large) with a single 2 GB file for maximum parallelism.
options.explanation: A larger warehouse cannot parallelize within a single large file — the bottleneck is file count, not warehouse size.

options.text: Split the 2 GB files into multiple smaller files of 100–250 MB compressed before loading.
options.explanation: Snowflake parallelizes loading by assigning individual files to different threads. Files that are too large cannot be split by a single COPY thread — the recommended file size is 100–250 MB compressed. Splitting the 2 GB files into ~20 smaller files each allows Snowflake to load them in parallel across warehouse nodes.

options.text: Convert files to JSON format before loading, as JSON enables better parallel processing in Snowflake.
options.explanation: JSON does not inherently enable better parallelism than CSV — file size is the key factor.

questions.stem: What is the purpose of the `SYSTEM$STREAM_HAS_DATA()` function in a Snowflake task?

options.text: It returns the number of rows in the stream waiting to be processed.
options.explanation: It returns a boolean, not a row count.

options.text: It forces the stream to materialize all pending changes to a staging table.
options.explanation: It does not materialize or process data.

options.text: It resets the stream offset to the beginning of Time Travel.
options.explanation: It does not reset the stream offset.

options.text: It returns TRUE if there is unconsumed change data in the stream, allowing a task to skip execution if the stream is empty.
options.explanation: `SYSTEM$STREAM_HAS_DATA()` is commonly used as a WHEN condition in a task definition to conditionally skip execution when there is no new data in the stream. It returns TRUE when the stream has unconsumed rows and FALSE when empty.

questions.stem: A data engineer wants to build a task DAG where `task_b` only runs after `task_a` completes successfully. Which DDL statement creates this dependency?

options.text: `CREATE TASK task_b AFTER task_a AS ...`
options.explanation: The `AFTER` clause in `CREATE TASK` establishes a dependency — task_b will be triggered after task_a completes successfully. Child tasks in a DAG do not have their own SCHEDULE; the root task's schedule drives the entire DAG.

options.text: `CREATE TASK task_b AFTER task_a SCHEDULE = '5 MINUTE' AS ...`
options.explanation: A child task in a DAG cannot have its own SCHEDULE — the schedule is only on the root task.

options.text: `CREATE TASK task_b DEPENDS ON task_a SCHEDULE = '5 MINUTE' AS ...`
options.explanation: DEPENDS ON is not valid Snowflake syntax for task dependencies.

options.text: `ALTER TASK task_b SET PREDECESSOR = 'task_a';`
options.explanation: SET PREDECESSOR is not a valid ALTER TASK option.

questions.stem: A data engineer writes a `MERGE INTO` statement to upsert data from a staging table into a target table. Which clause handles rows present in the staging table but NOT in the target table?

options.text: WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE
options.explanation: WHEN MATCHED applies when a row exists in both source and target (for updates).

options.text: WHEN NOT MATCHED BY SOURCE THEN DELETE
options.explanation: WHEN NOT MATCHED BY SOURCE handles rows in the TARGET that have no match in the source, typically used for deletes.

options.text: WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT
options.explanation: `WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT` handles rows in the source (staging) table that do not have a matching row in the target table — these are new rows that need to be inserted.

options.text: WHEN MATCHED BY SOURCE THEN INSERT
options.explanation: WHEN MATCHED BY SOURCE is not a valid Snowflake MERGE clause.

questions.stem: Which TWO statements correctly describe Snowflake Dynamic Tables? (Select TWO)

options.text: Dynamic tables can use FULL or INCREMENTAL refresh modes, with Snowflake selecting the method based on the query complexity.
options.explanation: and **C** are correct. Dynamic tables support both FULL refresh (recreating the entire result set) and INCREMENTAL refresh (processing only changed rows), with Snowflake selecting the appropriate method based on whether the query can be incrementally evaluated. Dynamic tables can also be chained — a downstream dynamic table can set `TARGET_LAG = DOWNSTREAM` to inherit its lag from the pipeline upstream.

options.text: Dynamic tables require a user-defined schedule in CRON format for refresh timing.
options.explanation: Dynamic tables use `TARGET_LAG` (a staleness duration), not a user-defined CRON schedule — Snowflake determines when to refresh.

options.text: Dynamic tables can be chained — one dynamic table's refresh can depend on another dynamic table's lag.
Dynamic tables are only supported in the Business Critical edition and above.
options.explanation: Dynamic tables are available in Enterprise edition and above, not only Business Critical.

questions.stem: A developer creates a JavaScript UDF in Snowflake. Which limitation applies specifically to JavaScript UDFs in Snowflake?

options.text: JavaScript UDFs cannot accept more than one argument.
options.explanation: JavaScript UDFs can accept multiple arguments.

options.text: JavaScript UDFs cannot be used in SELECT statements.
options.explanation: JavaScript UDFs can be called in SELECT, WHERE, and other clauses.

options.text: JavaScript UDFs cannot issue SQL queries (they cannot execute SQL inside the function body).
options.explanation: JavaScript UDFs in Snowflake are scalar functions that cannot execute SQL statements inside their function body — they can only process input arguments and return a value using JavaScript logic. If you need to execute SQL within a function, you must use a stored procedure instead.

options.text: JavaScript UDFs can only return INTEGER or VARCHAR data types.
options.explanation: JavaScript UDFs can return a variety of Snowflake data types, not just INTEGER or VARCHAR.

questions.stem: A data engineer needs an auto-incrementing surrogate key when inserting rows into a table. Which Snowflake feature provides this capability?

options.text: A column with IDENTITY property (e.g., id INT IDENTITY(1,1))
options.explanation: Snowflake supports the IDENTITY property on integer columns, which automatically generates sequential values for each inserted row. Sequences can also be used explicitly with `nextval`, but the IDENTITY column property is the most direct way to create an auto-incrementing surrogate key.

options.text: A stream with METADATA$ROW_ID
options.explanation: METADATA$ROW_ID is an internal Snowflake identifier in streams, not a user-accessible auto-increment key.

options.text: A task that calls NEXTVAL on insert
options.explanation: A task is not the mechanism for generating surrogate keys during inserts.

options.text: A materialized view with ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY 1)
options.explanation: ROW_NUMBER() in a materialized view does not generate persistent surrogate keys for new rows.

questions.stem: A data analyst accidentally deletes all rows from the `transactions` table at 10:00 AM. The table has a Time Travel retention of 1 day. Which query returns the table data as it existed at 9:55 AM?

options.text: `SELECT * FROM transactions BEFORE(TIMESTAMP => '2024-01-15 09:55:00'::TIMESTAMP_TZ);`
options.explanation: The syntax uses `BEFORE(TIMESTAMP => ...)` rather than `AT(TIMESTAMP => ...)`. The BEFORE clause returns data as of the instant just before the specified timestamp, not at it — and the timestamp given is '09:55:00', meaning it would return data from before 9:55 AM, not the state at 9:55 AM. The AT clause with a timestamp is the correct form.

options.text: `SELECT * FROM transactions AT(OFFSET => -300);`
options.explanation: `AT(OFFSET => -300)` queries the table as it existed 300 seconds (5 minutes) before the current time. If the query is run shortly after 10:00 AM, an offset of -300 seconds points to approximately 9:55 AM — precisely the window before the accidental deletion.

options.text: `SELECT * FROM transactions SNAPSHOT AT('09:55:00');`
options.explanation: `SNAPSHOT AT(...)` is not valid Snowflake Time Travel syntax.

options.text: `SELECT * FROM transactions HISTORY WHERE timestamp = '09:55:00';`
options.explanation: No `HISTORY WHERE timestamp` clause exists in Snowflake SQL.

questions.stem: A Snowflake data provider creates a SHARE and grants SELECT on a secure view to a consumer account. The consumer creates a database from the share. Which statement is TRUE about the shared data?

options.text: The consumer can see the DDL definition of the secure view since they have SELECT on it.
options.explanation: Secure views specifically hide the DDL from non-owners, including consumers.

options.text: Data is copied from the provider's account to the consumer's account when the shared database is created.
options.explanation: Data sharing explicitly does NOT copy data — the consumer reads the provider's live data.

options.text: The consumer accesses the provider's data in real time with no data movement; the provider's storage is read directly.
options.explanation: Snowflake Data Sharing provides zero-copy, live access to data. The consumer reads the provider's data directly from the provider's storage with no data movement or replication. This means changes in the provider's data are immediately visible to the consumer.

options.text: The consumer can write data back to the shared tables if they have INSERT privileges.
options.explanation: Shared objects are read-only from the consumer's perspective; consumers cannot write back to shared tables.

questions.stem: What is the minimum billing increment for a Snowflake virtual warehouse?

options.text: 1 minute
options.explanation: Snowflake bills warehouse compute per second with a minimum of 60 seconds (1 minute). If a warehouse runs for 10 seconds, you are charged for 60 seconds. After the first minute, billing increments per second.

options.text: 30 seconds
options.explanation: 30 seconds is below the minimum billing unit.

options.text: 1 hour
options.explanation: Hourly billing is used for some cloud services but not Snowflake virtual warehouses.

options.text: 5 minutes
options.explanation: 5 minutes is not the billing increment.

questions.stem: A multi-cluster warehouse is configured with MIN_CLUSTER_COUNT = 1 and MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT = 4, using Auto-Scale mode. When does Snowflake add a second cluster?

options.text: When total query execution time exceeds 5 minutes
options.explanation: Query execution duration does not trigger scaling.

options.text: When the warehouse has been running for more than 1 hour
options.explanation: Uptime does not trigger additional clusters.

options.text: When queries are queued due to insufficient concurrency on the running clusters
options.explanation: In Auto-Scale mode, Snowflake starts additional clusters when queries begin to queue up — meaning the existing clusters cannot service all incoming queries concurrently. The scaling policy (Standard or Economy) affects how quickly additional clusters are added, but the trigger is query queuing.

options.text: When the number of active sessions exceeds 10
options.explanation: Session count is not the scaling trigger; it is query queuing.

questions.stem: A company wants to run reporting queries and ETL jobs on the same Snowflake account without one workload impacting the other. What is the BEST architectural approach?

options.text: Create separate schemas for reporting and ETL and assign different file formats to each
options.explanation: Schemas control data organization, not compute isolation.

options.text: Increase the auto-suspend timeout so the warehouse is always available for both workloads
options.explanation: Using a single warehouse still allows workloads to compete for the same compute resources.

options.text: Use a single large warehouse and rely on query queuing to manage the workloads
options.explanation: Relying on queuing on a shared warehouse means one workload can block the other.

options.text: Use separate virtual warehouses for reporting and ETL so each workload has dedicated compute
options.explanation: Snowflake's compute/storage separation allows multiple virtual warehouses to access the same data simultaneously. Using separate warehouses for reporting and ETL is the recommended pattern for workload isolation — each workload gets dedicated compute resources and neither can slow down the other.

questions.stem: Which warehouse size doubles the number of compute nodes compared to a Large warehouse?

options.text: X-Large
options.explanation: Each warehouse size step doubles the compute (and credits per hour). A Large warehouse = 4 credits/hr. An X-Large = 8 credits/hr (double the nodes). 2X-Large would be four times a Large.

options.text: 2X-Large
options.explanation: 2X-Large is four times a Large, not double.

options.text: Medium
options.explanation: Medium is half a Large in compute.

options.text: 3X-Large
options.explanation: 3X-Large is eight times a Large.

questions.stem: The Query Acceleration Service (QAS) is enabled on a warehouse. Which type of query benefits MOST from QAS?

options.text: Queries with large hash joins between two tables of similar size
options.explanation: QAS does not specifically target hash joins between similarly-sized tables; it targets scan-heavy outliers.

options.text: Outlier queries with large scans that process far more data than the average query on that warehouse
options.explanation: The Query Acceleration Service is designed to improve the performance of outlier queries — queries that require scanning significantly more data than typical queries on that warehouse. QAS offloads parts of the scan to additional serverless compute, reducing the impact of these outlier queries on the warehouse.

options.text: Queries that are already hitting the result cache
options.explanation: Result cache hits do not execute on the warehouse at all.

options.text: INSERT statements loading data from an internal stage
options.explanation: Loading operations are not the target scenario for QAS.

questions.stem: Which TWO of the following actions will a resource monitor perform when a virtual warehouse reaches its defined credit quota? (Select TWO)

options.text: Drop all tables accessed by the warehouse
options.explanation: Resource monitors have no capability to drop tables.

options.text: Send a notification alert to specified users
options.explanation: and **C** are correct. A resource monitor can trigger two key actions: Notify (send email alerts to users when thresholds are reached) and Suspend Immediately (stop the warehouse and abort all running queries when the quota is hit). There is also a Suspend action that waits for current queries to complete before suspending.

options.text: Suspend the warehouse immediately, aborting running queries
Replicate the warehouse to a secondary region
options.explanation: Resource monitors do not initiate replication.

questions.stem: A multi-cluster warehouse is in Maximized mode with MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT = 3. All three clusters are running. When does Snowflake shut down one of the clusters?

options.text: In Maximized mode, all clusters run continuously and none are shut down unless the warehouse is manually suspended
options.explanation: In Maximized mode, all clusters are always running (up to MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT) for the entire time the warehouse is active. Clusters are not shut down based on load — the goal is maximum concurrency. Clusters are only shut down when the entire warehouse is suspended or manually stopped.

options.text: When one cluster has been idle for longer than the auto-suspend period
options.explanation: Auto-suspend applies to the warehouse as a whole, not individual clusters in Maximized mode.

options.text: When two consecutive clusters complete their queries without queuing
options.explanation: Maximized mode does not dynamically reduce cluster count based on query completion.

options.text: When the Economy scaling policy determines that one cluster has fewer than 5 active queries
options.explanation: The Economy scaling policy only applies to Auto-Scale mode, not Maximized mode.

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to run a single complex query that processes 10 TB of data. The query consistently spills to disk on a Large warehouse. What should they do to eliminate disk spillage?

options.text: Increase the AUTO_SUSPEND parameter to prevent the warehouse from suspending mid-query
options.explanation: Auto-suspend timeout does not affect memory availability during query execution.

options.text: Enable multi-cluster mode with MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT = 3
options.explanation: Multi-cluster warehouses improve concurrency by routing different queries to different clusters — they do not give a single query more memory.

options.text: Increase the warehouse size to provide more memory per node
options.explanation: Disk spilling occurs when a query requires more memory than is available on the warehouse nodes. Increasing the warehouse size provides more memory per node, allowing the query to process data in memory rather than spilling to local disk.

options.text: Add a clustering key to the table being scanned
options.explanation: While clustering can reduce scan volume, the issue of disk spilling for a 10 TB scan is a memory problem best solved by a larger warehouse.

questions.stem: What is the maximum Time Travel retention period available in Snowflake, and which edition is required?

options.text: 7 days; available on all editions
options.explanation: 7 days is the default on Enterprise, not the maximum, and it is not available on all editions at 7 days.

options.text: 90 days; requires Business Critical edition
options.explanation: Business Critical is not required — Enterprise edition suffices.

options.text: 90 days; requires Enterprise edition or above
options.explanation: The maximum Time Travel retention period is 90 days, and it requires Enterprise edition or above. Standard edition is limited to 1 day of Time Travel.

options.text: 30 days; available on Standard edition
options.explanation: Standard edition is limited to 1 day, not 30 days.

questions.stem: A developer accidentally drops a table with a 7-day Time Travel retention. How can they recover it?

options.text: SELECT * FROM TABLE(RESULT_SCAN(LAST_QUERY_ID())) to retrieve the dropped rows
options.explanation: RESULT_SCAN retrieves results of a previous query, not a dropped table.

options.text: UNDROP TABLE my_table; to restore the table from Time Travel
options.explanation: Snowflake's UNDROP TABLE command restores a dropped table (along with its data and metadata) within the Time Travel retention period. As long as the table was dropped within the last 7 days (per the retention setting), UNDROP TABLE restores it to its state at the moment of the drop.

options.text: CREATE TABLE my_table AS SELECT * FROM my_table AT(OFFSET => -1);
options.explanation: Referencing a dropped table in AT syntax would fail unless you use the table's ID or specific Time Travel reference.

options.text: Contact Snowflake Support — dropped tables cannot be restored by users
options.explanation: Users can self-serve with UNDROP TABLE — Snowflake Support is not required.

questions.stem: How does Snowflake's Fail-Safe period differ from Time Travel?

options.text: Fail-Safe data can be queried with SQL using AT or BEFORE clauses, unlike Time Travel
options.explanation: Fail-Safe is not accessible via SQL AT/BEFORE syntax.

options.text: Fail-Safe replaces Time Travel for Standard edition accounts
options.explanation: Fail-Safe exists alongside Time Travel (even when Time Travel is 0 days), not as a replacement.

options.text: Fail-Safe data is stored in a separate cloud region for disaster recovery
options.explanation: Fail-Safe data is not replicated to a separate region — it uses the same underlying storage.

options.text: Fail-Safe is a 7-day period (for permanent tables) after Time Travel expires; it is managed by Snowflake and not user-accessible via SQL
options.explanation: Fail-Safe is a 7-day emergency recovery window that begins after the Time Travel period expires for permanent tables. Unlike Time Travel, Fail-Safe data cannot be queried directly by users — it can only be accessed by Snowflake Support for disaster recovery purposes. It is not configurable and always lasts 7 days for permanent tables.

questions.stem: A developer creates a zero-copy clone of a 500 GB production table. How much additional storage is consumed immediately after cloning?

options.text: 500 GB — the data is duplicated into the clone
options.explanation: Zero-copy means no data duplication at clone time.

options.text: 250 GB — Snowflake stores a compressed copy
options.explanation: No compressed copy is made.

options.text: 0 GB — the clone shares the original micro-partitions with no data copied
options.explanation: Zero-copy cloning creates a clone that initially shares the exact same underlying micro-partitions as the source object. No data is copied at clone creation time, so the immediate additional storage cost is zero. Storage costs only arise for the clone when micro-partitions diverge due to DML operations on either the source or the clone (copy-on-write).

options.text: Approximately 1 GB — only the metadata is copied
options.explanation: While metadata is involved, the key point is that micro-partition data is not copied.

questions.stem: Which TWO of the following objects can be cloned using Snowflake's zero-copy clone feature? (Select TWO)

options.text: Database
options.explanation: and **C** are correct. Snowflake supports zero-copy cloning of databases, schemas, tables, stages, file formats, sequences, streams, tasks, and pipes. Cloning a database or schema creates a deep clone of all contained objects.

options.text: Network policy
options.explanation: Network policies are account-level configuration objects and cannot be cloned.

options.text: Schema
Resource monitor
options.explanation: Resource monitors are account-level objects and do not support cloning.

questions.stem: A table is set to DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0. What is the effect on Time Travel and Fail-Safe?

options.text: Time Travel is disabled (0 days); Fail-Safe still applies for 7 days after each DML operation
options.explanation: Setting DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0 on a permanent table disables Time Travel (you cannot query historical data), but Fail-Safe still applies. Fail-Safe is independent of the Time Travel setting and always provides 7 days of protection for permanent tables after each data change.

options.text: Time Travel is disabled; Fail-Safe is also disabled because the retention is 0
options.explanation: Fail-Safe is not disabled by setting retention to 0 on a permanent table.

options.text: Only the ACCOUNTADMIN can query historic data because retention is 0
options.explanation: No user (including ACCOUNTADMIN) can query historic data via SQL when retention is 0 — that is the Fail-Safe window only accessible via Snowflake Support.

options.text: The table automatically converts to a transient table with no Time Travel or Fail-Safe
options.explanation: The table remains a permanent table; it is not automatically converted to transient.

questions.stem: Which Snowflake feature provides continuous, automated loading of data from files arriving in a cloud storage location without a user-managed schedule?

options.text: COPY INTO with ON_ERROR = CONTINUE
options.explanation: COPY INTO requires explicit execution — ON_ERROR only controls error handling during a load.

options.text: Snowpipe
options.explanation: Snowpipe is Snowflake's continuous data ingestion service. It automatically detects and loads new files from a cloud storage location (using event notifications or REST API) as soon as they arrive, without a user-managed schedule.

options.text: A scheduled task running COPY INTO every minute
options.explanation: Scheduled tasks introduce polling-based latency and require user management.

options.text: External tables
options.explanation: External tables query data in place rather than loading it into Snowflake.

questions.stem: A data engineer executes COPY INTO my_table FROM @my_stage and 3 out of 100 files fail to load due to data errors. By default, what happens?

options.text: All 100 files are rolled back and no data is loaded
options.explanation: The default ON_ERROR behavior in Snowflake COPY INTO is ABORT_STATEMENT, which means that if any file fails, the entire COPY INTO statement is aborted and no data is loaded. To load successful files and skip failed ones, you must explicitly set ON_ERROR = CONTINUE.

options.text: The 97 successful files are loaded and the 3 failed files are skipped with errors reported
options.explanation: This describes the behavior of ON_ERROR = CONTINUE, not the default.

options.text: All 100 files are rejected until the errors are corrected
options.explanation: The default aborts the statement entirely rather than waiting for corrections.

options.text: The COPY INTO statement loads all 100 files and silently ignores any errors
options.explanation: Errors are never silently ignored — without ON_ERROR = CONTINUE, the statement aborts.

questions.stem: A data engineer creates an external stage pointing to an S3 bucket using: `CREATE STAGE my_ext_stage URL = 's3://mybucket/data/' CREDENTIALS = (AWS_KEY_ID = '...' AWS_SECRET_KEY = '...')`. What is a security concern with this approach?

options.text: External stages cannot use URL-based paths; they must use S3 ARNs
options.explanation: External stages do use URL-based S3 paths.

options.text: External stages can only be created by the ACCOUNTADMIN role
options.explanation: SYSADMIN and other roles with appropriate privileges can create stages.

options.text: Storing AWS credentials directly in the stage definition is insecure; Snowflake recommends using a storage integration with an IAM role instead
options.explanation: Embedding AWS access keys directly in a stage definition is a security risk — the keys are stored as objects in Snowflake and could be extracted. Snowflake's recommended approach is to use a Storage Integration, which creates a trust relationship between Snowflake and an AWS IAM role, eliminating the need to store long-lived credentials.

options.text: The CREDENTIALS parameter is deprecated and must be replaced with the ENCRYPTION parameter
options.explanation: CREDENTIALS is a valid parameter — it is just not the recommended approach for security reasons.

questions.reference: [Option 1: Configure a Snowflake storage integration to access Amazon S3](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-load-s3-config-storage-integration)

questions.stem: A data engineer notices that Snowpipe is not loading new files that arrive in S3. The pipe was working yesterday. Which is the FIRST diagnostic step they should take?

options.text: Drop and recreate the pipe to reset its state
options.explanation: Dropping and recreating the pipe would lose its load history metadata and could re-ingest already-loaded files.

options.text: Call SYSTEM$PIPE_STATUS('my_pipe') to check the pipe's execution state and error details
options.explanation: SYSTEM$PIPE_STATUS() returns diagnostic information about a Snowpipe, including its execution state (e.g., RUNNING, PAUSED, STOPPED_CLONED), whether there are errors, and details about the S3 event notification or SQS queue. This is the first diagnostic step.

options.text: Run COPY INTO manually to reload all files from the stage
options.explanation: Running COPY INTO manually loads files but does not diagnose why Snowpipe stopped.

options.text: Recreate the external stage to force a fresh S3 connection
options.explanation: The stage itself is unlikely to be the issue if the pipe was working previously.

questions.stem: Which TWO file formats does Snowflake support natively for loading semi-structured data? (Select TWO)

options.text: PARQUET
options.explanation: and **B** are correct. Snowflake natively supports loading data from CSV, JSON, Avro, ORC, Parquet, and XML file formats using COPY INTO. Parquet and Avro are commonly used semi-structured formats with native support.

options.text: AVRO
XLSX (Microsoft Excel)
options.explanation: Snowflake does not natively support loading XLSX files.

options.text: DOCX (Microsoft Word)
options.explanation: DOCX is a document format and is not supported for data loading.

questions.stem: A team needs to load 100 JSON files where each file contains an array of records at the root level (e.g., `[{...},{...}]`). After loading into a VARIANT column, they want one row per JSON object. Which COPY INTO option enables this?

options.text: STRIP_OUTER_ARRAY = TRUE in the FILE_FORMAT definition
options.explanation: When JSON files contain a top-level array, setting STRIP_OUTER_ARRAY = TRUE in the JSON file format causes Snowflake to strip the outer brackets and load each element of the array as a separate row. Without this option, the entire array would be loaded as a single VARIANT row.

options.text: FLATTEN = TRUE in the COPY INTO statement
options.explanation: FLATTEN is a SQL table function used in queries, not a COPY INTO option.

options.text: PARSE_HEADER = TRUE in the file format
options.explanation: PARSE_HEADER applies to CSV files for detecting column names in header rows.

options.text: ENABLE_OCTET_LENGTH_LIMIT = FALSE in the stage
options.explanation: ENABLE_OCTET_LENGTH_LIMIT is not a relevant option for this use case.

questions.stem: In Snowflake's RBAC model, which statement is TRUE about privilege inheritance?

options.text: Privileges are inherited from child roles to parent roles
options.explanation: Stating that privileges flow from child to parent is describing the grant direction, not the inheritance model. The question asks about how inheritance works: a parent role accumulates and inherits privileges from child roles granted to it.

options.text: Privileges are inherited from parent roles to child roles
options.explanation: In Snowflake's RBAC role hierarchy, when a child role is granted to a parent role, the parent role inherits all privileges held by the child role. A user who assumes the parent role therefore has access to all privileges granted to any role below it in the hierarchy.

options.text: Each role must be explicitly granted every privilege; inheritance does not apply
options.explanation: Privilege inheritance is a core and automatic feature of Snowflake RBAC — explicit re-granting is not required.

options.text: Privileges are inherited only if the INHERIT_PRIVILEGES flag is set on the role
options.explanation: INHERIT_PRIVILEGES is not a flag or parameter in Snowflake's RBAC model.

questions.stem: An administrator wants to prevent users from accessing Snowflake from outside the corporate IP range (10.0.0.0/8). Which sequence of commands is correct?

options.text: `CREATE NETWORK POLICY corp_policy ALLOWED_IP_LIST = ('10.0.0.0/8'); ALTER ACCOUNT SET NETWORK_POLICY = corp_policy;`
options.explanation: The proper Snowflake syntax is to first CREATE NETWORK POLICY with the ALLOWED_IP_LIST parameter, then apply it to the account with ALTER ACCOUNT SET NETWORK_POLICY. The ALLOWED_IP_LIST contains all permitted IP ranges; any connection from outside these ranges is denied.

options.text: `CREATE FIREWALL RULE corp_rule IP = '10.0.0.0/8'; APPLY RULE TO ACCOUNT;`
options.explanation: FIREWALL RULE is not valid Snowflake syntax.

options.text: `ALTER ACCOUNT SET ALLOWED_IPS = ('10.0.0.0/8');`
options.explanation: ALTER ACCOUNT SET ALLOWED_IPS is not a valid Snowflake command.

options.text: `CREATE NETWORK POLICY corp_policy DENY_LIST = ('0.0.0.0/0') ALLOW_LIST = ('10.0.0.0/8');`
options.explanation: The parameter names are ALLOWED_IP_LIST and BLOCKED_IP_LIST, not DENY_LIST and ALLOW_LIST.

questions.stem: A company needs to automatically provision Snowflake users and groups from their identity provider (Okta) without manually creating each user. Which Snowflake feature enables this?

options.text: SAML 2.0 SSO — once SSO is configured, users are automatically provisioned on first login
options.explanation: SAML SSO authenticates existing users but does not automatically create (provision) new Snowflake users without SCIM.

options.text: SCIM (System for Cross-domain Identity Management) — Snowflake's SCIM API syncs users and groups from the IdP
options.explanation: SCIM (System for Cross-domain Identity Management) is the protocol that enables automatic user and group provisioning from an identity provider like Okta or Azure AD to Snowflake. The IdP pushes create/update/deactivate operations to Snowflake's SCIM API, keeping users in sync.

options.text: OAuth 2.0 — OAuth tokens automatically create Snowflake users when authenticated
options.explanation: OAuth is an authorization protocol for access delegation, not user provisioning.

options.text: Key-pair authentication — RSA keys are synchronized between Okta and Snowflake automatically
options.explanation: Key-pair authentication is for service account authentication, not user provisioning from an IdP.

questions.stem: A column masking policy using SHA2 hashing is applied to the `ssn` column. An analyst runs a query joining the `customers` table (with masked SSN) to a `staging` table on the `ssn` column. What is the result?

options.text: The join succeeds and returns correct results because the masking is applied after the join predicate is evaluated
options.explanation: Masking is applied during query execution, affecting join comparisons.

options.text: The join returns no results because the hashed values in customers do not match the plain-text values in staging
options.explanation: A SHA2 masking policy replaces the SSN column value with its hash before the analyst sees it. When the analyst joins on the masked SSN column, they are comparing SHA2(ssn) in customers against the plain-text ssn in staging — these values will not match, so the join returns no rows. Masking is applied at query time before results are returned, and it affects join predicates as well.

options.text: Snowflake automatically unhashes the values to enable the join
options.explanation: Snowflake does not automatically reverse cryptographic hashing.

options.text: The query fails with a permission error because masked columns cannot be used in JOIN conditions
options.explanation: The query does not fail with a permission error — the column is queryable, just masked.

questions.stem: Which TWO features does Snowflake's column-level security encompass? (Select TWO)

options.text: Dynamic Data Masking — masking sensitive column values based on the querying role
options.explanation: and **B** are correct. Snowflake's column-level security features include Dynamic Data Masking (which replaces or obfuscates column values based on role) and External Tokenization (which integrates with third-party tokenization solutions to replace sensitive values with tokens).

options.text: External Tokenization — replacing sensitive values with tokens managed by an external tokenization provider
Row Access Policies — filtering rows based on user attributes
options.explanation: Row Access Policies are a row-level security feature, not column-level security.

options.text: Column Encryption at Rest — encrypting specific columns with customer-managed keys
options.explanation: Snowflake encrypts all data at rest by default using AES-256, and Tri-Secret Secure is the customer-managed key feature — but this is not categorized as column-level security.

questions.stem: Which Snowflake role can grant privileges on account-level objects such as warehouses and databases to other roles?

options.text: Only ACCOUNTADMIN can grant privileges on any object
options.explanation: The object owner does not need to be ACCOUNTADMIN — any owning role can grant privileges on objects they own.

options.text: The owner of an object (the role that created it) can grant privileges on that object to other roles
options.explanation: In Snowflake's discretionary access control (DAC) model, the role that owns an object (created it) is its owner and can grant privileges on that object to other roles. For example, if SYSADMIN creates a warehouse, SYSADMIN can grant USAGE on that warehouse to other roles.

options.text: Any role with USAGE privilege can grant that privilege to others
options.explanation: USAGE privilege does not grant the ability to grant that privilege to others — only the object owner (or a role with GRANT OPTION) can re-grant.

options.text: SECURITYADMIN can grant any privilege on any object
options.explanation: SECURITYADMIN manages users and roles but does not automatically have privileges on all objects.

questions.stem: Which Snowflake data type is used to store JSON, Avro, ORC, Parquet, and XML data in a single column?

options.text: OBJECT
options.explanation: OBJECT is a Snowflake type for key-value maps, which is a subset of what VARIANT can hold.

options.text: ARRAY
options.explanation: ARRAY is a Snowflake type for ordered arrays, also a subset of VARIANT.

options.text: VARIANT
options.explanation: The VARIANT data type is Snowflake's universal semi-structured data type. It can store any valid semi-structured value including JSON objects, arrays, strings, numbers, booleans, and null — as well as data loaded from Avro, ORC, Parquet, and XML files.

options.text: JSON
options.explanation: JSON is not a Snowflake column data type — VARIANT is used to store JSON data.

questions.stem: A VARIANT column `v` contains `{"user": {"name": "Alice", "age": 30}}`. Which query correctly extracts the value `Alice`?

options.text: `SELECT v['user']['name'] FROM my_table;`
options.explanation: Snowflake supports bracket notation for navigating VARIANT paths: `v['user']['name']` traverses the 'user' key and then the 'name' key, returning the string 'Alice'. This is the standard, unambiguous form tested on the SnowPro exam.

options.text: `SELECT v.user.name FROM my_table;`
options.explanation: is incorrect in this context because dot notation (`v.user.name`) can conflict with column aliasing when a column is named the same as a JSON key, making it ambiguous. Bracket notation is the safe and recommended form for nested VARIANT access.

options.text: `SELECT GET(v, 'user.name') FROM my_table;`
options.explanation: `GET(v, 'user.name')` looks for a top-level key literally named 'user.name' (with a dot in the key name), not a nested path. To use GET for nested paths, you must chain calls: `GET(GET(v, 'user'), 'name')`.

options.text: `SELECT EXTRACT(user.name FROM v) FROM my_table;`
options.explanation: EXTRACT is not a valid Snowflake function for VARIANT path navigation.

questions.stem: A developer has a VARIANT column containing an array of strings: `["red","green","blue"]`. They want one row per color value. Which SQL pattern accomplishes this?

options.text: `SELECT f.value FROM my_table, LATERAL FLATTEN(INPUT => my_table.colors) f;`
options.explanation: LATERAL FLATTEN is the Snowflake function for expanding arrays (and objects) in VARIANT columns into multiple rows. The LATERAL keyword allows FLATTEN to reference the current row's column. The output column `f.value` contains each array element as a separate row.

options.text: `SELECT UNNEST(colors) FROM my_table;`
options.explanation: UNNEST is a PostgreSQL/BigQuery function that does not exist in Snowflake's SQL dialect.

options.text: `SELECT SPLIT(colors, ',') FROM my_table;`
options.explanation: SPLIT returns an array, not multiple rows, and the input is already a VARIANT, not a delimited string.

options.text: `SELECT PARSE_JSON(colors) FROM my_table;`
options.explanation: PARSE_JSON parses a string into VARIANT — the data is already in VARIANT form.

questions.stem: A developer queries a VARIANT column with `SELECT v:price::FLOAT FROM orders`. For some rows, the `price` key is missing. What does Snowflake return for those rows?

options.text: An error is raised because the path does not exist
options.explanation: Missing VARIANT paths do not raise errors.

options.text: 0.0 is returned as the default FLOAT value
options.explanation: Snowflake does not substitute default type values — it returns NULL.

options.text: NULL is returned for rows where the key does not exist
options.explanation: When a VARIANT path does not exist for a particular row, Snowflake returns SQL NULL rather than an error. The colon path syntax (`v:price`) gracefully returns NULL for missing keys, and casting NULL::FLOAT still yields NULL. This is a key advantage of Snowflake's schemaless VARIANT handling.

options.text: The query skips rows where the key is missing
options.explanation: The query processes all rows; rows with missing paths simply return NULL.

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about schema detection for semi-structured data in Snowflake are TRUE? (Select TWO)

options.text: INFER_SCHEMA can automatically detect column names and types from Parquet and JSON files in a stage
options.explanation: and **C** are correct. INFER_SCHEMA is a Snowflake table function that reads staged files (Parquet, JSON, Avro, ORC) and detects the column names and data types. The result can be used with CREATE TABLE ... USING TEMPLATE (SELECT ARRAY_AGG(OBJECT_CONSTRUCT(*)) FROM TABLE(INFER_SCHEMA(...))) to automatically create a typed table matching the file's schema.

options.text: INFER_SCHEMA works on CSV files by reading the first row as a header
options.explanation: INFER_SCHEMA works on semi-structured binary formats like Parquet; for CSV, PARSE_HEADER = TRUE is used in a file format, but INFER_SCHEMA is not the tool for CSV.

options.text: The USING TEMPLATE clause in CREATE TABLE AS SELECT can use the output of INFER_SCHEMA to create a typed table
Schema detection requires Business Critical edition because it accesses external storage
options.explanation: INFER_SCHEMA is available on all Snowflake editions.

questions.stem: A developer notices a query is returning results instantly even though the underlying table was just updated. Which Snowflake caching layer is MOST likely responsible?

options.text: The local disk (SSD) cache on the warehouse nodes
options.explanation: The local disk cache reduces remote storage I/O but does not return results instantly without warehouse compute.

options.text: The query result cache in the Cloud Services layer
options.explanation: The query result cache stores the results of recently executed queries in the Cloud Services layer. If the exact same SQL (with the same role) is re-executed and the underlying data has not changed within 24 hours, the result is returned instantly from the cache without touching the warehouse.

options.text: The metadata cache in the Cloud Services layer
options.explanation: The metadata cache stores table and partition statistics, not full query results.

options.text: The materialized view refresh cache
options.explanation: Materialized view refresh is a background operation, not a caching mechanism that serves query results.

questions.stem: The Search Optimization Service is enabled on the `events` table. For which query pattern does it provide the GREATEST benefit?

options.text: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM events GROUP BY event_type
options.explanation: GROUP BY aggregations scan many rows and do not benefit from point-lookup optimization.

options.text: SELECT * FROM events WHERE event_id = 'abc-123'
options.explanation: The Search Optimization Service is designed to accelerate equality-based point lookup queries — queries that search for a specific value in a column (e.g., looking up a single event_id). It builds a persistent search access path that allows Snowflake to find the relevant micro-partitions without a full table scan.

options.text: SELECT * FROM events ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 100
options.explanation: ORDER BY with LIMIT is a top-N query pattern, not a point lookup.

options.text: SELECT AVG(duration) FROM events WHERE event_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-12-31'
options.explanation: A date range scan across a year of data is a range query, not the point-lookup pattern that Search Optimization is designed for.

questions.stem: A query's profile shows the step 'Bytes Spilled to Local Storage'. What does this indicate about the query execution?

options.text: The warehouse is writing intermediate results to the Snowflake stage for backup
options.explanation: Spilling is not related to backup or stages.

options.text: The query required more memory than was available on the warehouse nodes, causing intermediate data to be written to local SSD
options.explanation: 'Bytes Spilled to Local Storage' in the Query Profile indicates that the warehouse ran out of in-memory space for an operator (typically hash joins or sorts) and had to write intermediate data to the local SSD disk. This is slower than in-memory processing but faster than spilling to remote storage. When you see this, the typical remedy is to increase the warehouse size to provide more memory.

options.text: The query is reading data from an external stage, which is classified as 'local storage' by the profiler
options.explanation: External stage reads show up differently in the profile.

options.text: The query result was too large to fit in the query result cache and was spilled to local storage
options.explanation: The result cache handles query results, and result size does not cause local spilling.

questions.stem: Which TWO actions help improve partition pruning efficiency for a large table that is frequently queried by date range? (Select TWO)

options.text: Define a clustering key on the date column so data is co-located by date in micro-partitions
options.explanation: and **B** are correct. Defining a clustering key on the date column tells Snowflake to organize micro-partitions so that rows with the same date values are co-located. When data is well-clustered on date, a date range query can eliminate the vast majority of micro-partitions. Enabling automatic clustering ensures that as new data is inserted (which arrives in natural insertion order), Snowflake automatically reorganizes the micro-partitions to maintain good clustering on the date column.

options.text: Enable automatic clustering on the table so Snowflake continuously reorganizes micro-partitions by date
Increase the warehouse size to improve partition scanning speed
options.explanation: Increasing warehouse size improves compute resources but does not change how data is physically organized in storage — it does not improve pruning.

options.text: Enable the Search Optimization Service for the date column
options.explanation: The Search Optimization Service improves point lookups, not range scans on date columns.

questions.stem: A Snowflake data provider wants to share a table with a consumer account in the same cloud region. What is the FIRST object the provider must create to enable sharing?

options.text: A Reader Account
options.explanation: Reader Accounts are for consumers who do not have Snowflake accounts — they are an optional feature, not the first step.

options.text: A Data Exchange listing
options.explanation: A Data Exchange listing is a higher-level marketplace abstraction built on top of shares.

options.text: A SHARE object
options.explanation: The SHARE object is the fundamental building block of Snowflake Data Sharing. The provider creates a SHARE, grants privileges on the objects to be shared (databases, schemas, tables, secure views) to the SHARE, and then grants the SHARE to the consumer account.

options.text: A Secure View
options.explanation: A Secure View may be added to a share, but the SHARE object must be created first.

questions.stem: A company wants to share Snowflake data with a business partner who does not have a Snowflake account. What should the provider create to enable access?

options.text: An external stage with public S3 permissions so the partner can download files
options.explanation: Sharing via public S3 bypasses Snowflake's governance and security features.

options.text: A Reader Account — a Snowflake-managed account provisioned by the provider for the consumer
options.explanation: A Reader Account (also called a Managed Account) is a Snowflake account created and managed by the data provider specifically for consumers who do not have their own Snowflake account. The provider pays for the Reader Account's compute and manages it. The consumer gets access only to the shared data.

options.text: A Business Critical account on behalf of the partner
options.explanation: You cannot create a Business Critical account on behalf of a partner — Reader Accounts are the correct mechanism.

options.text: An OAuth integration that generates tokens for the partner's application
options.explanation: OAuth integration is for authentication, not for creating access for non-Snowflake consumers.

questions.stem: A data provider shares a table with a consumer. The provider inserts 10,000 new rows into the shared table. When does the consumer see the new data?

options.text: After the provider manually triggers a share refresh
options.explanation: No manual refresh is needed — sharing is live.

options.text: After a 15-minute synchronization delay
options.explanation: There is no synchronization lag in Snowflake's native data sharing.

options.text: Immediately — the consumer reads live data from the provider's storage with no synchronization lag
options.explanation: Snowflake Data Sharing provides live, real-time access to the provider's data. The consumer reads directly from the provider's underlying micro-partitions — there is no data copy and no synchronization delay. Changes committed by the provider are immediately visible to the consumer.

options.text: After the consumer drops and recreates their shared database
options.explanation: The consumer does not need to recreate their database; they see the live data automatically.

questions.stem: A provider wants to share a view that joins two internal tables and applies a business-logic filter. Why MUST the provider use a secure view instead of a regular view?

options.text: Regular views cannot be added to a SHARE; only secure views are allowed
options.explanation: is partially true (regular views cannot be added to a share), but the reason is B's explanation about security.

options.text: Secure views prevent the consumer from seeing the view definition (DDL), protecting proprietary business logic, and they are required for sharing to prevent data leakage through query plan optimization
options.explanation: is correct for two reasons: first, secure views hide the underlying DDL from consumers who are not the owner, protecting the provider's business logic; second, regular views in Snowflake perform query optimization that can inadvertently leak information about underlying data through the query plan — secure views disable certain optimizations to prevent this. Both properties make secure views mandatory for data sharing.

options.text: Secure views compress data before transmitting it to the consumer account
options.explanation: Secure views do not compress data.

options.text: Regular views do not support filtering, so a secure view is needed for the business logic
options.explanation: Regular views fully support filtering.

questions.stem: A consumer account mounts a shared database. Which statement about what the consumer can do with the shared data is TRUE?

options.text: The consumer can INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE rows in the shared tables
options.explanation: Shared tables are read-only to the consumer — INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE are not permitted.

options.text: The consumer can CREATE TABLE AS SELECT from shared tables to create local copies
options.explanation: While shared tables are read-only (the consumer cannot modify them), the consumer can run SELECT queries against them and can CREATE TABLE AS SELECT to copy shared data into local tables in their own account. This is a common pattern for consumers who want to manipulate or transform the data.

options.text: The consumer cannot run any queries against shared data — they can only view the schema
options.explanation: Consumers can fully query shared objects with SQL.

options.text: The consumer can only access the data using Python; SQL queries are not supported
options.explanation: Shared data is accessible via any Snowflake interface including SQL, Python, and other clients.

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about the Snowflake Data Marketplace are TRUE? (Select TWO)

options.text: Data Marketplace listings use the same underlying SHARE mechanism as direct data sharing
options.explanation: and **C** are correct. The Snowflake Data Marketplace (now called Snowflake Marketplace) is built on Snowflake's native data sharing technology — listings are backed by SHARE objects. Consumers get a shared database that points to the provider's live data, with no data copy or movement.

options.text: All data available on the Marketplace is free to access for any Snowflake account
options.explanation: While some listings are free, others are paid and require a subscription.

options.text: Consumers do not receive a copy of the data; they query the provider's live data via a shared database
Providers must be on Business Critical edition to publish a listing to the Marketplace
options.explanation: Providers do not need Business Critical edition — any edition can publish to the Marketplace.

questions.stem: A provider account is on AWS US-East-1. A consumer wants to access the shared data but is on Azure West-Europe. Which statement about cross-cloud sharing is TRUE?

options.text: Cross-cloud and cross-region sharing is not supported; both accounts must be on the same cloud and region
options.explanation: Cross-region sharing IS possible but requires replication as described.

options.text: Snowflake automatically replicates the shared data to Azure West-Europe at no additional cost
options.explanation: Snowflake does not automatically replicate shared data — the provider must set up replication.

options.text: Cross-cloud and cross-region sharing requires the provider to use database replication to create a secondary database in a region close to the consumer, which is then shared
options.explanation: Direct Snowflake data sharing works within the same cloud region. For cross-cloud or cross-region access, the recommended approach is for the provider to replicate their database to a Snowflake account in the consumer's cloud/region, and then share from that replicated database. This requires enabling database replication (Enterprise edition or above) and incurs replication and cross-region egress costs.

options.text: The consumer must create an external stage on AWS to act as a transfer point
options.explanation: External stages are not the mechanism for cross-cloud sharing.

questions.stem: A provider creates a SHARE and grants SELECT on `db.schema.sensitive_table` to the share. They notice they forgot to add the database-level privilege. What is the correct sequence of grants?

options.text: GRANT USAGE ON DATABASE db TO SHARE my_share; GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA db.schema TO SHARE my_share; GRANT SELECT ON TABLE db.schema.sensitive_table TO SHARE my_share;
options.explanation: Snowflake data sharing requires a hierarchical grant sequence: first grant USAGE on the DATABASE, then USAGE on the SCHEMA, then SELECT on the specific TABLE or VIEW. Without USAGE on the database and schema, the consumer cannot navigate to the table.

options.text: GRANT SELECT ON TABLE db.schema.sensitive_table TO SHARE my_share; (table-level SELECT is sufficient)
options.explanation: Table-level SELECT alone is insufficient — the consumer also needs database and schema USAGE to access the object.

options.text: GRANT ALL ON DATABASE db TO SHARE my_share;
options.explanation: GRANT ALL on a database to a share is overly permissive and not how sharing works; you only share what you intend.

options.text: GRANT USAGE ON SHARE my_share TO ACCOUNT consumer_acct;
options.explanation: GRANT USAGE ON SHARE grants the share to a consumer account, which is a separate step after the object grants.

questions.stem: What is a Data Exchange in Snowflake?

options.text: A command-line tool for exchanging files between Snowflake stages and local machines
options.explanation: File exchange between stages and local machines is handled by PUT/GET commands, not a Data Exchange.

options.text: A private hub where a community of Snowflake accounts can share data with each other in a controlled environment
options.explanation: A Snowflake Data Exchange is a private, invitation-only data sharing marketplace.
